claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 5 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 21 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 13 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 21, which is 20 or over
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 5 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 21, which is 20 or over
circumstances: Child is 21, which is 20 or over
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Charlie
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 13 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To start, I need to know about your child. 

**How old is your child?**

This is important because eligibility depends on the child's age:
- Children under 16 generally qualify
- Children aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children aged 20 or over don't qualify

Please tell me your child's age.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I've actually got three kids I'm asking about.

My youngest, Alex, is 5 years old. Then there's Charlie who's 13, and my oldest, Blake, just turned 21.

Do you need to check each of them separately, or how does this work?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Yes, I do need to check each child separately as they have different eligibility criteria based on their ages. Let me work through each of your three children:

1. **Alex (5 years old)** - Under 16
2. **Charlie (13 years old)** - Under 16  
3. **Blake (21 years old)** - Over 20

Let me start by checking the eligibility for each child based on their age category.

**For Blake (21 years old):**
Unfortunately, children aged 20 or over do not qualify for Child Benefit, so Blake would not be eligible.

**For Alex (5 years old) and Charlie (13 years old):**
Since they're both under 16, they may qualify. However, I need to ask a few questions about each of them.

Let me start with **Alex (5 years old)**:

**Is Alex working full-time (16 or more hours per week)?**

(This would be unusual for a 5-year-old, but I need to confirm this as part of the eligibility check.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Oh no, Alex isn't working at all - they're only 5! They're in primary school.

And Charlie isn't working either - they're still at school as well, just doing regular school stuff.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that makes sense! Since neither Alex nor Charlie are working full-time, let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Let me navigate properly through the assessment for Alex first:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to continue checking a few more criteria for Alex and Charlie:

**For both Alex (5) and Charlie (13):**

**Are either of them receiving any benefits in their own right?** 

This would include benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit that are claimed in the child's own name (not yours as the parent). This is quite rare for children their age.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receives any benefits in their own name. They're just kids - any benefits we get would be in my name, not theirs.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's what I would expect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check:

**Are Alex or Charlie doing any apprenticeships?**

Again, this is unlikely for their ages (5 and 13), but I need to confirm. Certain types of apprenticeships can affect eligibility for older teenagers.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not. Alex is only 5 and Charlie is 13 - they're way too young for apprenticeships. They're both just in regular school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since neither of them are in apprenticeships, let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about **your** residency status as the claimant:

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

If you do normally live in the UK, that's the main residency requirement. If you don't normally live in the UK, there are some special circumstances that might still allow you to claim (such as being a Crown servant abroad, or having certain types of settled or pre-settled status).

So, do you normally live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. We're in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since you normally live in the UK, you meet the residency requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check about your responsibility for the children:

**Do you live with Alex and Charlie?**

Or if you don't live with them, do you contribute to their upkeep (such as paying for their food, clothes, housing, etc.)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, both Alex and Charlie live at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since you live with both Alex and Charlie, you meet the responsibility requirement for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check:

**Is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Charlie?**

For example, another parent, a grandparent, or another guardian? Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them. It's just me looking after them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about where Alex and Charlie are living:

**Are either Alex or Charlie:**
- In hospital?
- In local authority care?
- Being fostered?
- Or are they living normally at home with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Well, Alex is living normally at home with me - no issues there.

But with Charlie, it's a bit complicated. They've been in care for a couple of months now, but they come home to stay with me on the weekends.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for explaining that - I understand this can be a difficult situation. Let me check the rules for children in care, as there are specific time limits and conditions.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Let me handle each child separately since they have different situations.

**For Alex (5 years old)** - who is living normally at home:

Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?

(I expect the answer is no, but I need to confirm)

**For Charlie (13 years old)** - who has been in care for a couple of months:

Since Charlie is in care, I need to check the specific rules for this situation. Let me go back and navigate through Charlie's situation properly.

First, let me confirm about Alex - is the council paying anything towards Alex's care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
No, the council isn't paying anything for Alex. I'm just looking after them myself at home.

For Charlie, yes - they're in local authority care, so the council is covering that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Let me now properly assess Charlie's situation since they are in care. Let me start a fresh assessment path for Charlie.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, now I understand the rules for children in care. Let me ask you about Charlie's specific situation:

**For Charlie (13 years old) who has been in care for a couple of months:**

You mentioned Charlie comes home on weekends. The rules state that:
- Child Benefit continues for up to **8 weeks** if a child is in care
- OR if the child spends at least **24 hours per week** at home with you

You said Charlie has been in care for "a couple of months" - can you tell me:

1. **Exactly how many weeks has Charlie been in care?** (Is it more than 8 weeks?)
2. **How much time does Charlie spend at home with you each week?** (You mentioned weekends - is this at least 24 hours per week?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Charlie has been in care for about 4 weeks now - so less than 8 weeks.

And yes, they come home every weekend - easily more than 24 hours. They arrive Friday evening and go back Sunday afternoon, so it's probably around 48 hours or so each week.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Thank you for that clarification. This is really helpful.

**For Charlie (13 years old):**
- Charlie has been in care for 4 weeks (which is less than the 8-week limit)
- Charlie spends around 48 hours per week at home with you (which is well over the 24-hour minimum)

So Charlie meets the exception rules for children in care - you can still claim Child Benefit for them.

Now, just to complete Charlie's assessment:

**Is the local council paying towards Charlie's accommodation or maintenance while they're in care?**

(I believe you mentioned earlier that the council is covering Charlie's care, so I expect the answer is yes - but this is important for determining eligibility.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, the council is paying for Charlie while they're in care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. This is an important factor.

Since the local council is paying for Charlie's care, unfortunately **you cannot claim Child Benefit for Charlie** while they remain in this arrangement, even though Charlie spends weekends at home with you. The rule is that you generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child's accommodation or maintenance.

However, the good news is that **you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex (5 years old)**, as:
- Alex is under 16
- Not working
- Not receiving benefits in their own right
- Not in an apprenticeship
- You live in the UK
- You live with Alex and are responsible for them
- Nobody else is claiming for Alex
- Alex lives at home normally
- The council is not paying for Alex

**Regarding Blake (21 years old):** Unfortunately, Blake is not eligible as children aged 20 or over do not qualify for Child Benefit, regardless of their circumstances.

Let me provide you with the formal assessment outcome:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Summary of Your Child Benefit Eligibility

Based on our assessment, here's the outcome for each of your three children:

---

### ✅ **Alex (5 years old) - ELIGIBLE**
You **can claim** Child Benefit for Alex. They meet all the eligibility requirements as they are under 16, living at home with you, and the council is not paying for their care.

---

### ❌ **Charlie (13 years old) - NOT ELIGIBLE (currently)**
You **cannot claim** Child Benefit for Charlie at this time because the local council is paying for their accommodation and maintenance while they're in care. 

**Important:** This may be temporary. If Charlie's care arrangement changes (for example, if they return home permanently or the council stops paying for their care), you may be able to claim Child Benefit for them again at that point.

---

### ❌ **Blake (21 years old) - NOT ELIGIBLE**
You **cannot claim** Child Benefit for Blake because they are over 20 years old. Child Benefit is only available for children under 16, or aged 16-19 if they're in approved education or training.

---

### **Next Steps:**

1. **For Alex:** You can apply for Child Benefit. You can do this online or by post using form CH2. Visit: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

2. **For Charlie:** Keep HMRC informed of any changes to Charlie's care situation. If Charlie returns home permanently or the council stops funding their care, contact HMRC to update your claim.

3. **Income considerations:** Be aware that if you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, the High Income Child Benefit Charge may apply, though you can still claim Child Benefit.

Do you have any questions about this assessment or what to do next?

reasonings: Alex (5 years old) meets all eligibility criteria: under 16 years old, not working full-time, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in a disqualifying apprenticeship, lives at home with the claimant in the UK, claimant is responsible for them, no other person is claiming Child Benefit for them, and the local council is not paying towards their accommodation or maintenance.

Charlie (13 years old) does not qualify because the local council is paying for their accommodation and maintenance while they are in care. Although Charlie has been in care for less than 8 weeks and spends 24+ hours per week at home (which would normally allow continued eligibility), the fact that the council is paying for their care means Child Benefit cannot be claimed during this period.

Blake (21 years old) does not qualify because they are over the age limit. Child Benefit is only available for children under 16, or aged 16-19 if they are in approved education or training. Children aged 20 or over are not eligible regardless of their circumstances.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for one of their three children (Alex). Charlie is temporarily ineligible while in local authority care with council funding, though eligibility could resume if circumstances change. Blake is permanently ineligible due to age.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.question: Has the child been in care for 8 weeks or less, OR do they spend at least 24 hours a week at home?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 8 weeks in care. Exception if child spends 24+ hours/week at home. Can claim again if child spends 2 consecutive nights/week or 7+ days in a row at home.

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (5 years old) meets all eligibility criteria: under 16 years old, not working full-time, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in a disqualifying apprenticeship, lives at home with the claimant in the UK, claimant is responsible for them, no other person is claiming Child Benefit for them, and the local council is not paying towards their accommodation or maintenance.

Charlie (13 years old) does not qualify because the local council is paying for their accommodation and maintenance while they are in care. Although Charlie has been in care for less than 8 weeks and spends 24+ hours per week at home (which would normally allow continued eligibility), the fact that the council is paying for their care means Child Benefit cannot be claimed during this period.

Blake (21 years old) does not qualify because they are over the age limit. Child Benefit is only available for children under 16, or aged 16-19 if they are in approved education or training. Children aged 20 or over are not eligible regardless of their circumstances.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for one of their three children (Alex). Charlie is temporarily ineligible while in local authority care with council funding, though eligibility could resume if circumstances change. Blake is permanently ineligible due to age.
